Justification: The study fits a first order multivariate autoregressive model to observed time series of species level catch data from a stationary fish trap spanning 1950 to 1974 and uses log transformed CPUE as the state variable. It states that Ives et al. 2003 derive three measures of stability from MAR1 models that characterise the community in terms of its resilience reactivity and resistance. It defines resilience as a measure of return time of the community from an ecological disturbance or displacement from equilibrium and quantifies it from the leading eigenvalue of the community matrix B. The CPUE data are species level catch data weighed in kg and aggregated into three trophic groups so the target variable is biomass at the community trophic group level. The approach combines field observation data with a community matrix model and stability analysis. The first author affiliation is Dalhousie University Halifax NS Canada. The data come from a trap anchored to the seabed 200 m from the Camogli coast of the Ligurian Sea off the coast of Italy. The figure describes the trap located off the coast of Camogli Italy within the Ligurian Sea. Environmental forcing is represented as process errors interpreted as environmental forcing and the discussion refers to increased temperature and stochastic forcing so the disturbance is treated as unmanipulated environmental forcing over the time series. The analysis spans 25 years from 1950 to 1974 so the observation duration is 25 years.

The study states that it integrates US Drought Monitor classifications with MODIS-derived NDVI indices for vegetation resistance and recovery and it assesses vegetation health across 2010 to 2014. It defines resistance as Rs = NDVI 2011 divided by NDVI 2010 and recovery as Rc = NDVI 2012 or 2013 or 2014 divided by NDVI 2011 which directly uses observed NDVI values. It explicitly says the concepts of vegetation resilience namely resistance and recovery were quantitatively assessed using indices derived from MODIS NDVI data which supports the framework resilience = resistance + recovery. The core modeled analysis uses OLS regression with a focus on vegetation resistance Rs as the dependent variable which makes the resilience quantification used in the main analysis resistance. NDVI is used to represent vegetation health and is described as maintaining greenness during and after drought events reflecting resistance and recovery which is an ecosystem function proxy. The study area is the Lower Mississippi River Basin covering portions of Missouri Illinois Kentucky Tennessee Arkansas Mississippi and Louisiana in the United States. The drought events are described as naturally occurring drought events of varying severity which indicates an unmanipulated climatic disturbance. The study period is described as spanning from March 2 2010 to June 25 2013 and it analyzes a continuous time window to capture overlapping drought and recovery periods which treats drought as a press type driver. The first author affiliation is Appalachian State University in the United States. The riparian systems are described as including floodplains wetlands and bottomland hardwood forests and wetlands are explicitly included as a major riparian system. The response variable is riparian vegetation NDVI which indicates plants.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + stress
Justification: The study developed a regional ecosystem health assessment framework described as a “Vigor-Organization-Resilience-Stress” VORS framework and computed EHI as EVI×EOI×ERI×ESI. Ecosystem resilience was not quantified from recovery dynamics but was computed as ERI = sum Ai×RCi where RCi is an ER coefficient assigned by land use type and taken from previous research. The ER coefficients are described as calculated by a weighted average of stability and resilience with weights of 0.6 and 0.4 and are provided in Table 2 which indicates a proxy based construction rather than observed recovery. The approach uses RS and GIS with MOD13A1 NDVI and other spatial datasets for Guizhou Province from 2000 to 2020 which is field observation via remote sensing and spatial data products. The first author affiliation is Guizhou University in China. The study area is Guizhou Province China with coordinates 24•37′-29•13′N and 103•36′-109•35′E and the location provided is the midpoint of these bounds. The resilience related target variable is land use composition through Ai proportions and land use type coefficients which is a landscape level variable. Habitat type cannot be reduced to a single listed IUCN category because the analysis spans multiple land use types including cropland forestland grassland water construction land and bare land so the major habitat type is coded as Other. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable under the instructions.

The study defines interaction network resilience and then states that it calculated bee and plant network resilience using the network resilience parameter βeff. The parameter βeff is described as a one-dimensional mathematical model quantifiable from an ecological network and it defines the state of an ecosystem against disturbances such as loss in interactions species interaction weight or combinations. The analysis uses plant-bee interaction networks pooled across four sampling sessions per plot and does not quantify recovery dynamics after a disturbance event. The methods describe field surveys that recorded plant-bee pollinator visitation frequencies and assembled weighted interaction matrices and then computed βeff. Because resilience is quantified from a network parameter rather than from observed recovery of a state variable through time the measurement type is inferred. The introduction links mutualistic interactions to ecosystem stability and discusses network resilience within that context. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the interaction network expressed through βeff derived from the plant-bee interaction matrix. The data are community level because they involve multiple bee species and multiple plant species in interaction networks. The approach is field observation because the methods state they recorded visitation frequencies on 50 study plots across seasons. The first author affiliation lists the International Centre of Insect Physiology and Ecology in Nairobi Kenya. The study area is in Kenya in the Eastern Afromontane Biodiversity Hotspot with coordinates given for Taita Hills and Murang’a County. The average coordinate is based on the midpoints of the reported coordinate ranges. The habitat description emphasizes regrowth vegetation formed as a result of subsistence farming and grazing and includes cropland and built-up areas and plantations so the dominant habitat type is agricultural. Taxa whose resilience was quantified are plants and bees.

The study applied controlled trampling treatments with lanes receiving 0 to 500 passes and one lane as a control and it measured vegetation response immediately before trampling and about 2 weeks after trampling and 1 year after trampling. Resilience is defined as the ability of a vegetation type to recover following the cessation of trampling and the resilience index is the change in relative vegetation cover during the year after trampling expressed as a fraction of the change in cover that occurred 2 weeks after trampling. The response variable used to compute resistance resilience and tolerance is relative vegetation cover which is based on estimated cover of each vascular plant species and of mosses and lichens summed to total cover and adjusted for changes on controls. The framework explicitly separates vulnerability into resistance resilience and tolerance and states that the response of vegetation to trampling is expressed in terms of three indices resistance tolerance and resilience. The quantification for resilience uses change over time in cover during the year after trampling relative to the initial change which is a recovery rate type measure rather than a time to recovery. The target variable is cover because indices are calculated from relative vegetation cover values. The level is community because cover is summed across species to obtain total cover for each vegetation type and resilience is calculated for each vegetation type. The approach is field experiment because experimental study sites were established in five mountainous regions and trampling treatments were administered in early summer with follow up field measurements. The first author affiliation is Aldo Leopold Wilderness Research Institute Forest Service US Department of Agriculture in Missoula Montana. The field country is the United States because sites were established in five different mountainous regions of the United States and states listed include Washington Montana Colorado New Hampshire and North Carolina. The disturbance is manipulated because trampling passes were applied by the researchers and it is biotic because it is human trampling. The disturbance pattern is pulse because trampling was administered as discrete passes and recovery was measured after cessation. Observation duration is 365 days because follow up measurements were taken 1 year after trampling.

The study states it uses the “Vigor-Organization-Resilience (VOR) model” and that “EH is ecosystem health；EV is ecosystem vigor；EO is ecosystem organization；ER is ecosystem resilience” with the model “EH = EV×EO×ER”. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics but from land use areas and coefficients because Table 3 defines “ER = Σ Ai×RCi” where “Ai is the area of land use type i” and “RCi is the ecosystem elasticity coefficient of land use type i which is derived by combining the expert scores and previous studies”. This matches an inferred measurement because resilience is constructed from proxy land use and expert derived coefficients rather than observed recovery. The target variable used to quantify resilience is land use area and land use based coefficients as given in the ER formula. The study area is “Jiaozhou Bay… in Qingdao Shandong Province” and the first author affiliation is “Ocean University of China Qingdao… China” so institution country and field country are China. The coastal zone is defined as “the townships within 10 km of the coastline” around Jiaozhou Bay so the habitat is coastal. The approach relies on land use data remote sensing images and POI data and does not describe an experiment so it is field observation. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from Jiaozhou Bay Qingdao. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The introduction defines resilience as a function of resistance and recovery stating the resilience of natural systems can be considered as a function of resistance and recovery. The methods compile observational coral cover data before and after disturbances stating a global data set of total coral cover before and after acute storms temperature stress and coastal run off from flooding events. Disturbance impact is quantified as change in coral cover using the log response ratio of pre to post disturbance percentage coral cover. Recovery is quantified from time series coral cover after disturbance by fitting linear models and extracting the slope as annual recovery rate in the 5 year period following disturbance. The study therefore uses observed coral cover trajectories to quantify resilience. The target variable used for recovery rate is percentage coral cover. The major taxa studied are scleractinian hard corals which are animals. The location is described as tropical coral reefs globally and the results describe a database spanning 54 degrees of latitude so the field country and location are global. The disturbances are acute storms and temperature stress events and run off from flooding events which are treated as short lived events so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The recovery rate window is five years following disturbance so the observation duration is 1825 days. The first author affiliation is the University of Western Australia in Australia.

The study fits models to daily river gross primary production time series and uses a latent biomass state Bt with an intrinsic growth rate rmax. It states that the model allows estimation of maximum autotrophic biomass growth rates rmax as a proxy for the resilience of primary producers to flood disturbances. It also states that streams have relatively high ecosystem resilience defined as fast recovery in algal biomass and primary productivity. Resilience is quantified as the median value of the low density maximum growth rate rmax and converted to daily maximum percent increase in biomass as (ermax−1)×100. This is a recovery rate because it is a growth rate describing how quickly biomass increases after disturbance. The disturbance is flood high flow because the model estimates effects of flow disturbance on biomass persistence Pt as a function of discharge Qt and discusses reductions in GPP following flood disturbances. The disturbance is unmanipulated because it uses observed discharge and GPP time series rather than experimental manipulation. The disturbance is treated as pulse because it refers to high flows on a specific date and recovery in the week following the disturbance. The approach is modeling and simulation because both LB TS and S TS are state space models fit using Bayesian inference in Stan. The first author affiliation is University of Nevada Reno in the United States. The field data are from six rivers in the United States including sites in WV MD GA OK CA and TX. The observation duration is two consecutive years because the study selected six rivers with 2 y time series and fit the first year then predicted the second year.

The study used outdoor experimental stream channels and imposed simulated spates by increasing current to about 60 cm/s and scouring tiles with a high pressure hose. Resilience was assessed using post spate recovery dynamics because the authors state resilience is the ability to return to a reference state and in this system the reference was convergence with a temporally changing undisturbed control community. The authors assessed resilience in terms of biomass parameters specifically cell numbers and in terms of community composition using DCA ordination trajectories. They report disturbed communities reaching biomass and taxonomic structure similar to controls after 3 to 9 d which provides a recovery time measure. The target variables used for resilience quantification were cell densities and diatom assemblage composition. The work was a field experiment at the University of Louisville experimental stream facility in Bernheim Forest Nature Preserve Bullitt County Kentucky USA drawing water from Harts Run. The first author affiliation is University of Louisville Louisville Kentucky USA. The habitat is a mixed hardwood forest basin described for Harts Run. The study subjects were lotic algal communities including diatoms and filamentous green algae. The disturbance was manipulated and hydrological because it was a simulated spate high discharge event. The disturbance pattern was pulse because each spate was imposed for 2 h and then flow returned to pre disturbance levels. The observation window for the experiment was 33 d from the initial disturbance on 27 April 1988 to day 33.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the ability of a system to absorb changes to maintain the same identity and states that functional evenness has been used as a proxy for the resilience of communities. The discussion states that non-invaded showed higher resilience i.e. FEve than invaded communities and later states that invaded community diversity is more affected by and thus has a lower resilience to disturbance. The methods quantify diversity using species richness FDiv FEve and MPD and compare these metrics between non-invaded invaded and invaded no alien communities. The study does not quantify recovery dynamics after a disturbance and instead infers resilience from functional evenness and other diversity metrics. The study area is the Tagus estuary area Portugal and Figure 1 shows the study area around the Tagus Estuary Portugal. The first author affiliation is University of Lisbon Lisboa Portugal. Bird point counts were conducted at 189 points in 2021 to 2022 indicating field observation. The focal subjects are bird communities and the metrics are community level diversity metrics. Disturbance is discussed as human disturbance and human modification but resilience quantification is not based on a defined disturbance event response so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not specified. Land classes include forest agriculture and urban and wetland areas so the dominant habitat type cannot be assigned to a single IUCN category from the resilience quantification alone and is coded as Other.

Target variable type: Land use; Ecosystem function; Abiotic parameter; Socio-eco parameter; Regional network parameter
Justification: The paper states it developed an ecological resilience assessment process chain across the disturbance carrying recovery dimensions and named it the D-C-R three-dimensional process model chain with ecological disturbance force ecological carrying capacity and ecological recovery capacity. Ecological resilience is quantified as an index ERI using a weighted formula ERI = ωdi EDF + ωci ECC + ωri ERC with EDF ECC and ERC computed from multiple indicators and weights. The indicators used include land use vulnerability land reclamation rate distance from construction land population density nighttime lights GDP distribution road network density NDVI NPP habitat quality water conservation soil conservation carbon sequestration and landscape metrics such as patch cohesion and fragmentation. This is an indicator based assessment and does not quantify recovery dynamics of a state variable after a defined disturbance event so resilience is inferred. The study area is the Dongting Lake region located in the middle reaches of the Yangtze River spanning Hunan and Hubei provinces and the case study cities are Yueyang Changde Yiyang and Jingzhou. The first author affiliation is Central South University Changsha Hunan China. The analysis uses remote sensing and GIS with raster data standardized to 1 km by 1 km and maps ER from 2000 to 2020 which is consistent with field observation via remote sensing rather than an experiment or imposed disturbance. The habitat focus is a water land transition zone centered on Dongting Lake with river and lake floodplains so Wetland is the dominant habitat type. No coordinates are provided in the text so the location is approximated to the Dongting Lake region center and the range is set to 0 because only one average coordinate is reported.

The study quantified stability using NDVI time series because it states the assessment was based on NDVI from 2001 to 2022 and NDVI was chosen as an indicator to reflect changes in ecosystem status. Resilience was quantified from the ARx coefficient alpha because the methods state the coefficient alpha characterizes the resilience of an ecosystem and a larger absolute value implies a slow rate of returning to an equilibrium state from a disturbance. This corresponds to recovery rate because the text explicitly links alpha to the rate of returning to equilibrium and to vegetation memory via NDVI t and NDVI t-1. The framework is ecosystem stability = resistance + resilience because the introduction states resistance and resilience are the two most fundamental aspects of ecosystem stability and defines resilience as recover its original state after disturbances and resistance as maintain its original state unchanged under external disturbances. The approach is field observation because the core data are remote sensing NDVI and gridded hydroclimatic datasets and the study analyzes observed anomalies rather than imposing experimental manipulations. The disturbance types are unmanipulated climatic and hydrological because the study analyzes hydroclimatic anomalies including precipitation anomaly and potential evapotranspiration anomaly and soil moisture anomaly as external disturbances. The disturbance pattern is press because exposure is defined by the frequency of extreme hydroclimatic events during the growing season over 2001 to 2022 with no defined recovery interval and resilience is estimated from continuous monthly anomaly dynamics. The field location is ARNWC with coordinates 73E to 107E and 34N to 49N and the provided location is the midpoint of that range. The habitat type is desert because the study area is described as arid with most areas sparsely vegetated due to severe water scarcity and land desertification and desert vegetation is a major biome and dominates large areas. The taxon is Plantae because the focal observed variable is vegetation NDVI and the biomes are vegetation types including shrubs meadows steppes forests and desert vegetation. The institution country is CN because the first author affiliation is Lanzhou University Lanzhou China. The observation duration is 2001 to 2022 which is 22 years and is converted to 8030 days.

The introduction states that the study considers engineering resilience as the return time of individual plant species in a community to the equilibrium after abrupt change events. The methods describe PBM simulation experiments where groundwater levels and salinities were altered once in the simulated time series for one year after a 50 year burn in and then conditions returned to the previous level for 100 simulation years. The methods state that they determined the return time as the number of years for which control and treatment runs differed significantly after the change event. The framework text contrasts resistance and resilience and states that the PBM simulates response to salt and water stress as resistance as well as regenerative and reproductive processes as resilience. The occurrence measure used for PBM based resilience analysis is species specific relative frequency defined as the proportion of runs in which a given species was present. The study uses model simulations and treats the PBM predictions as true and corrects SDM predictions accordingly. The illustrative example study site is Michaelsdorf with coordinates 12.56 E and 54.36 N at the German Baltic Sea. The first author affiliation is Technische Universitaet Braunschweig in Germany. The disturbance is imposed in the model as abrupt temporary changes of mean annual groundwater level and salinity from one year to the next. The change event duration is one year and is treated as a discrete event so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window for resilience in the PBM experiments spans 50 years burn in plus 1 year disturbance plus 100 years recovery which totals 151 years.

The introduction defines resilience as rate of recovery by stating greater resilience rate of recovery of more species rich assemblages. The methods state that they conducted a 27 month field experiment assessing algal recruitment and succession in cleared patches that mimic naturally forming gaps and that they surveyed patches monthly estimating the cover of all algal species. The statistical analysis states to analyze recovery rates we compared the slope of perennial algal cover over time among treatments and examined the interaction between time and treatments to assess the effect of treatments on recovery rates. This directly quantifies resilience from observed recovery dynamics in cover so the measurement is observed and the quantification is recovery rate. The study system location is given as Bodega Marine Reserve on the northern coast of California USA with coordinates 3881901200N 123 8402400W which converts to N38.3195 W123.6733. The disturbance is imposed by scraping the rock surface within the patches bare of all visible algae and by randomly assigning an herbivore reduction treatment using barriers and removal of mobile invertebrates so it includes manipulated structural disturbance and manipulated biotic disturbance. The disturbance is a discrete clearing event in May 2008 followed by recovery monitoring so the pattern is pulse. Observation duration is from May 2008 to 27 months later which is 27 times 30 days equaling 810 days. The first author affiliation is Center for Population Biology University of California Davis California USA so the institution country is US. The focal recovering community is a macroalgal community and the target variable used for recovery rate is perennial algal cover so the level is community and the taxon is Plantae. The habitat is a mid to high rocky intertidal zone which fits coastal.

The introduction frames the study as anticipating resilience through understanding population responses to recurrent disturbances. The methods quantify short term potential and long term performance using integral projection models parameterized with annual surveys of 3171 tagged coral colonies between 2016 and 2019 in southern Japan and eastern Australia. The paper defines damping ratio as a measure of demographic recovery describing the rate at which a population perturbed from its stationary equilibrium converges back to its asymptotic growth trajectory. This makes the resilience measurement observed because it is quantified from the modeled response of population state variables derived from observed survival growth fragmentation fecundity and recruitment data. The quantification category is recovery rate because damping ratio is explicitly described as the rate of convergence back to the asymptotic growth trajectory and the instructions state autocorrelation like rate measures fall under recovery rate. The target variable type is demography parameter because resilience is quantified using demographic recovery and transient dynamics metrics derived from survival development reproduction and recruitment. The level is population because the IPMs and resulting metrics describe population level characteristics such as asymptotic population growth and demographic recovery. The approach includes field observation because the study used repeated annual surveys of tagged colonies and also includes modeling and simulation because it used IPMs to quantify demographic recovery and other transient metrics. The first author affiliation is University of St Andrews in the United Kingdom. The field countries are Japan and Australia because the surveys were in southern Japan and eastern Australia. The methods provide GPS coordinates for the four regions and the average of those coordinates is S1.1 E141.4 with all locations within two degrees of that average in both latitude and longitude so the location range is 0. The habitat is shallow coral reef communities so Marine.Neritic best matches the IUCN options. The taxon is Animalia because the subjects are corals. Disturbance is unmanipulated climatic because the study discusses recurrent disturbances and uses sea surface temperature regimes and abiotic variability as the disturbance context without experimental manipulation. The disturbance pattern is press because the abiotic variability is treated as continuous exposure using 1950 to 2019 SST time series and not as a discrete event with a recovery interval. Observation duration is 1095 days because surveys were conducted between 2016 and 2019.

The methods state that they aimed "to approximate the resilience of each lineage" by modeling "current and future distribution of suitable habitats expressed as the number of climatically suitable cells" using SDMs. This quantifies resilience from projected habitat suitability rather than from observed recovery dynamics of the system. The study uses climatic variables and SDM projections under CMIP5 future scenarios and reports percent loss or gain of "climatically suitable cells" for 2050 and 2070. No time series of population state variables or post disturbance recovery is measured and the disturbance is not imposed as an experimental event but represented through future climate scenarios. The framework is not defined as an equation including resilience and the paper discusses resilience conceptually as "potential resilience" and "resilience to climate change" without specifying components. The first author affiliation is "Univ. of Zurich Zurich Switzerland". The study system distribution and sampling span multiple European regions stated as "Alps Carpathians Iberia and northern Europe" and the lineages are referred to as "CW Alps western Carpathians Scandinavia and Pyrenees". The habitat description includes "calcareous fens edges of streams or lakes and drier calcareous grasslands" and wetland fens are explicitly named. The taxon is a plant because the focal species is "the cold adapted plant Primula farinosa".

Framework: resilience = capacity to remain within a stability domain or state
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the capacity of a system to remain within a stability domain or state and states that the size of the region between the desired stable state and the separatrix represents the resilience of the stable state. The methods quantify resilience in the deterministic model as the size of the area between the lower stable equilibrium and the unstable equilibrium where these co occur and state that stochastic shocks that push the system across the unstable boundary will result in a system within the basin of attraction of the upper equilibrium value. The results describe flip up and flip down points and critical P input rates for transitions between low and high chlorophyll states. This matches a threshold magnitude of disturbance without shifting to an alternative state which corresponds to latitude. The response variable used in the model is areal chlorophyll and the paper treats chlorophyll as an ecosystem level variable linked to algal abundance and lake productivity. The study uses a nonlinear model and stochastic simulations drawing driver values and calculating equilibrium chlorophyll and resilience across 1000 trials. The first author affiliation is Center for Limnology University of Wisconsin Madison Wisconsin USA. Data sources include northern Wisconsin lakes and experimental lakes in northern Michigan and a regression for small lakes in Ontario Canada. The lake district is identified as the Northern Highlands Lake District Wisconsin and the plate describes northern Wisconsin. The disturbance considered in resilience calculations is changing phosphorus input and other drivers in the model which is a resource change treated as a continuous driver across simulations rather than a discrete event. The resilience calculations are based on equilibrium solutions rather than a time series so the duration between first and last measurement for resilience is not provided.

The study defines high environmental quality urban areas partly by avian communities with high potential resilience to face ecological stress. The methods state that potential resilience of bird assemblages was estimated by combining the species richness and redundancy of functional traits. The potential community resilience was computed as a combination of species richness and the functional evenness FEve index calculated from 73 traits. This is a proxy based construction and not a direct measurement of recovery dynamics after a disturbance so the measurement type is inferred. The introduction defines ecological resilience as capacity to absorb environmental changes without altering its main functions but the methods do not operationalize resilience as recovery rate recovery time or recovery degree. The resilience related target variables used in quantification are species richness and functional redundancy via FEve which is a functional trait distribution metric. The bird community at each point count was defined as the list of bird species recorded during the visit indicating a community level. Data were collected by standardized point counts in 15 cities in 10 European countries during the 2018 breeding season indicating field observation. The first author affiliation is Czech University of Life Sciences Prague in the Czech Republic. The table lists the surveyed cities and countries including Czechia Estonia Spain Finland France Greece Hungary Italy Netherland and Poland. The study context is within cities and urban areas and the classification is for urban areas so the habitat type is urban. The study subjects are birds which are Animalia. No disturbance is imposed or measured for resilience quantification because resilience is treated as potential resilience and used in the HEQ classification.

The paper proposes a quantitative measure called Persistence Index to assess the capacity of communities to maintain their functions and services after disturbances and it states that PI is based on the diversity abundance and redundancy of disturbance and taxon specific response traits. The index is described as quantifying adaptive capacity based on the assumption that an ecosystem will be more resilient and resistant if it contains a greater presence of species with a given set of response traits. Resilience is defined as the ability to undergo disturbance without shifting to an alternative state and resistance is discussed as a complementary concept and the text says assessing persistence requires to consider resilience and resistance as complementary concepts. PI is calculated from a species specific response trait matrix with presence or absence of response traits and a vector of species relative abundance derived from number of trees per hectare and forest cover. The case study uses the Third Spanish National Forest Inventory forest plots across Peninsular Spain and the Balearic Islands and calculates PI for each plot and compares PI across forest types and species richness. The study uses Monte Carlo simulations and also uses national forest inventory data. The response traits include resprouting ability stimulated recruitment hard and downy leaves seed bank longevity rooting depth and wood density which are traits linked to resistance and or resilience to drought fire and windthrow. No time series of ecosystem state recovery is measured and resilience is assessed through trait based indicators rather than observed recovery dynamics. The study area is Peninsular Spain and the Balearic Islands and the first author affiliation is in Solsona Spain.

Framework: resilience = distance to the boundary of the attraction basin
Justification: The paper states that it mathematically defines resilience as the distance to the boundary of the attraction basin. It also states that it compares distances to tipping points across different systems on the basis of data on abundance and topology and that in most cases we only have abundance and network data reflecting mere snapshots. The Methods section states that it used proportional abundance and network topology and that the method makes it possible to obtain the tipping points without relying on time-series data. Because resilience is derived from snapshot abundance and network topology rather than measured recovery dynamics from observational time series it is inferred. The study uses simulations of species dynamics using equation 1 and simulated perturbations on networks and converts systems into a one-dimensional function. The target variables used for the resilience comparison are abundance and network topology including mutualistic strength gamma derived from adjacency matrices. The model is for mutualistic interactions between plants and animals and uses plant and animal abundances. The first author affiliation is Shanghai Jiao Tong University in China. The text explicitly names locations including Nahuel Huapi National Park in Argentina and Morne Seychellois National Park Mahé in the Seychelles and Hickling and also cites Chiloé Island Chile and Tenerife Canary Islands Spain as sources of abundance networks. The text does not provide explicit latitude and longitude values in the provided excerpt and therefore the coordinates cannot be reported.

The abstract states ecological threshold is used to facilitate evaluation of ecosystem resilience and the study derives sea level rise thresholds for salt marsh stability. Methods state that spatial patterns of salt marshes were quantified by 26 landscape metrics predicted from a mechanistic wetland change model and thresholds were derived from sigmoid fits and from the first principal component. The resilience related conclusion is that using total areas alone may provide a biased interpretation that salt marshes are more resilient to sea level rise than they actually are which indicates resilience is evaluated via proxy indicators rather than measured recovery. The Methods describe model simulations under sea level rise rates from 4 to 20 mm per year for 2050 and 2100 and do not describe observing real recovery dynamics of salt marsh state variables through time. The study area is Grand Bay National Estuarine Research Reserve in Mississippi United States with coordinates 30.37 N 88.43 W. The first author affiliation is The University of Southern Mississippi in the United States. The habitat is extensive salt marshes which are coastal wetlands. Because resilience is assessed through landscape metrics and principal components rather than direct observed recovery it is inferred. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience is provided in the introduction methods or discussion.

The study states it constructed a Drought Vulnerability Index integrating exposure sensitivity and resilience as three core dimensions defined in the IPCC framework and it defines VI as integrating EI SI and RI. Sensitivity and resilience were derived from autoregressive modeling of vegetation dynamics using vegetation and climate data from 2001 to 2023. The autoregressive model uses standardized NDVI as the system state variable and δ captures the autoregressive component and serves as an indirect measure of ecosystem resilience and the ecosystem resilience RI is expressed as 1 minus δ. Autoregressive dependence implies recovery dynamics and the text states a larger δ suggests slower recovery and therefore lower resilience which makes the resilience quantification a recovery rate type. NDVI is a vegetation index and the paper treats it as vegetation dynamics and greenness and productivity so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The disturbance is drought represented by PDSI and described as a climatic hazard and it is not experimentally manipulated so it is unmanipulated climatic. The disturbance is treated across a long continuous monthly record rather than a single imposed event so it is press. The study area is the Yangtze River Basin in central China with coordinates 28 N to 35 N and 90 E to 122 E and the average is reported. The first author affiliation is Nantong University in China. The observation window is monthly data from 2001 to 2023 which spans 22 years or 8030 days using 365 days per year. The basin includes multiple vegetation types including forests shrublands savannas grasslands wetlands and croplands so a single IUCN habitat type cannot be uniquely assigned from the text.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + services
Justification: The study states it quantitatively assessed ecosystem health based on the Vigor – Organization – Resilience – Services VORS model. Ecosystem resilience is defined as the ability of an ecosystem to recover from external disruptions and is operationalized as a coefficient RCi calculated from NDVIi NDVI_mean j and a land class restoring force coefficient RCj with fixed values for grassland building land cropland forestland water and unutilized land. This uses land use classes and assigned coefficients rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance. The paper reports spatial patterns and differences among land use types such as farmland construction land woodland and unused land and does not present a disturbance event with observed recovery time series for resilience. The framework explicitly places resilience as one component within ecosystem health via VORS. Data sources include MODIS NDVI and landcover and other spatial datasets and the study area is Fujian Province in China. The first author affiliation is Qufu Normal University in Rizhao China. The habitat context is dominated by forest because Fujian is described as a significant forested region with a forest coverage rate of 66.8% and forestland shows high ecosystem health in results. No coordinates are provided in the text for the study area maps. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable.

The study states that it measured success using the ability of restored vegetation to recover from disturbance and it tested resilience by simulating a physical disturbance through clipping of all above ground cordgrass biomass and removal of mussels. It then measured cordgrass survival and regrowth 10 days and 13 months after this disturbance. These are direct measurements of system response after an imposed disturbance so the measurement type is observed. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience is provided in the introduction or methods beyond discussing growth and resilience as criteria so the framework is none. Resilience is quantified by post disturbance survival and regrowth and the results report higher percentage of plots still harbouring vegetation and higher numbers of shoots after disturbance which reflects degree of recovery at specified times rather than a recovery rate or recovery time. The target variables used for resilience are cordgrass survival and regrowth measured as emergence and number of shoots. These are demographic type variables and are measured for cordgrass transplants across plots which corresponds to a population level response. The work is a full factorial field experiment conducted on Sapelo Island Georgia USA at latitude 31.40749167 and longitude −81.28987601 in a saltmarsh high marsh platform which fits the coastal habitat type. The first author affiliation is Radboud University Nijmegen The Netherlands. The disturbance is manipulated and structural because the disturbance is created by clipping vegetation and removing mussels. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event in August 2014 with follow up measurements so the pattern is pulse. The observation duration for resilience is from the disturbance to the last resilience measurement 13 months later which is 395 days using 30 days per month.

The study used long term observational and consecutive ANPP data at least 10 years from 36 studies. Extreme climatic events were classified as extreme drought years and extreme wet years using SPEI and were counted as years in which ECEs occurred. The paper defines resilience as the level of productivity relative to its undisturbed state after an ECE and resistance as the magnitude of the response of productivity to ECEs. Resistance and resilience were calculated following Isbell et al. 2015 using Yn as average yearly ANPP across all non ECE years and Ye and Ye+1 as ANPP during an ECE year and a year following an ECE. The resilience equation uses Ye Yn and Ye+1 Yn which quantifies post ECE productivity relative to the undisturbed state. The target variable used in resilience calculation is yearly ANPP which is described as aboveground net primary productivity and community productivity. The major taxa studied are herbaceous plant and the system is natural grassland. The locations are described as 22 sites in Asia and 14 sites in North America including alpine grassland on the Tibetan Plateau and North American prairie and an example site is Haibei China. The first author affiliation is Lanzhou University Lanzhou China. The disturbance is climatic and unmanipulated because ECEs are identified from observed climate indices rather than imposed. ECEs are treated as discrete years and resilience uses the year following an ECE indicating a pulse disturbance framing. The time period is 1980s to 2010s but the exact first and last measurement dates for each study are not provided so observation duration cannot be calculated.

The study analyzed a long term vegetation record from the Sonoran Desert from 1953 to 2009 and used changes in plant species cover to assess resilience concepts. The data were from 68 permanent transects where absolute cover of perennial plants was measured repeatedly and communities were identified based on species composition. Table 1 states that diagonal entries represent the percentage of occasions where a sample did not undergo transition and indicate resilience which operationalizes resilience as remaining in the same community. Transitions were tracked between successive measurements and compositional dissimilarity was calculated with Bray Curtis index. The study linked transition frequency and species dissimilarity to precipitation in the 12 months preceding sampling and reported positive correlations with precipitation. The transects did not experience direct vegetation manipulations and the analysis focused on autogenic changes in relation to climatic drivers. The first author affiliation is Texas A and M University in the United States. The field site is the Santa Rita Experimental Range in Arizona USA and the map and text identify it as near Tucson Arizona. The disturbance considered in the resilience analysis is climatic variation in precipitation treated across the record rather than as a single event. The observation window spans 56 years from 1953 to 2009.

The paper states that it uses a scaled extended Klausmeier reaction diffusion model and that it employs numerical simulations to assess conservation strategies. Climate change is represented as decreasing rainfall a with scenarios of slow and fast change and simulations use a(t)=0.5(1-t/T) with T=10^5 or T=10^4 and another simulation decreases a from 1 to 0 in 10^6 time steps or 10^3 time steps. Resilience is treated as the amount of environmental change a system can withstand before it flips to another regime as described in Box 3 where ecological resilience is determined by a-ac,N. This corresponds to a threshold magnitude before a patterned state becomes unfeasible and shifts to another state which matches latitude. The disturbance is modelled decreasing rainfall which is climatic and it is applied continuously over the simulation window which is press. The target variable used to quantify resilience in Box 3 is rainfall a relative to the critical value ac,N which is an abiotic parameter. The study concerns vegetation patches and vegetation biomass v in a dryland ecosystem model so the taxon is plants. No new data were used and no empirical field site coordinates are provided so field country and location are NA. The first author affiliation is Leiden University in the Netherlands so institution country is NL. The longest stated simulation duration is 10^6 time steps so observation duration is 1000000 days as the only explicit start to end count provided.

Framework: UEH = pressure + vigor + organization + resilience + service
Justification: The paper proposes the Pressure-Vigor-Organization-Resilience-Service modeling framework and states that UEH is the target layer which consists of five criterion layers P V O R S. Resilience is not measured as recovery after a disturbance but is defined as ability to restore and is expressed in terms of habitat quality HQ. HQ is calculated from land use maps and threat factors using an InVEST style habitat quality formulation rather than observed recovery trajectories. The UEHI is computed by aggregating normalized indicators with weights and the indicators include HI La FVC SHDI CONTAG AI AWMPFD HQ and ESV. The data sources are Landsat images DEM geographic information and socio-economic data and the method uses land use classification and landscape metrics which are proxy indicators. The first author affiliation is Fujian Normal University in China. The study areas are Changle in China and Suez in Egypt. Changle is located at 25 40 N to 26 04 N and 119 23 E to 119 59 E and Suez is located at 29 50 N to 30 02 N and 32 26 E to 32 35 E. The study is an urban ecosystem health assessment for coastal cities and uses remote sensing and spatial analysis rather than experiments. Because the resilience quantification is based on indicator system proxies and not on observed recovery dynamics the measurement type is inferred and disturbance fields are not applicable.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the degree and rate of recovery of a community from a perturbation. The methods state that the study quantified the recovery of the native plant communities resilience and soil nutrient levels following experimental disturbances. The methods define resilience as the rate and degree of native community recovery from disturbance. Community recovery was measured as the degree to which native cover and richness metrics in disturbed plots matched levels measured in adjacent undisturbed plots in each post treatment year. Cover and species richness of all plant taxa were estimated to assess community responses to disturbance including resilience. The disturbance was experimentally imposed by digging up and breaking the soil to a depth of approximately 15 cm and removing plant biomass including roots and shoots. Treatments were initiated at the end of the growing seasons and plots were read for recruitment during peak growing season the year following treatment and cover was also estimated in the second post treatment year for plots initiated in year 1. The results report that native plant cover in disturbed plots remained depressed in Turkey and MT but had recovered to undisturbed levels in LP two years after treatment indicating very high resilience to disturbance in this community. Study areas are given with centroids for Turkey 38.0 and 29.1 Montana 47.0 and 113.5 and La Pampa minus 37.3 and minus 64.6. The first author affiliation includes Rocky Mountain Research Station United States Forest Service Missoula Montana USA.

Framework: stability = resilience + asymptotic stability + basin width
Justification: The paper quantifies stability and related resilience concepts using stochastic differential equation models and quasi-potential analysis rather than field data. It states that the quasi-potential provides a way to quantify stability in stochastic systems and that Holling called the height of the basin of attraction resilience. It also identifies three metrics associated with the quasi-potential including curvature basin width and basin height and presents them together in Fig. 5 as stability concepts. Because resilience is explicitly described as basin height and basin width is described as latitude in the provided definitions the closest quantification option is Latitude. The disturbance is continual stochastic perturbations described as Gaussian white noise added to the models and the paper emphasizes noise occurs continually with low intensity which matches a press pattern. The disturbance is modeled as stochastic perturbations to nutrient input and to resource and consumer state variables so the manipulation level is model and the disturbance type best matches climatic because it is described as environmental perturbations and stochasticity in nutrient input. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study uses stochastic differential equations simulations and numerical computation of quasi-potentials using Camerons algorithm and the QPot package. The first author affiliation is Case Western Reserve University Cleveland OH USA so the institution country is US. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is based on classic models and simulations. The realization in example 3 is shown over time interval 0 to 50 and the paper also discusses steady state limits t to infinity but the only explicit observation window shown is 0 to 50 and example 1 uses dt 0.005 and example 2 uses dt 0.025 without explicit total time so the only explicit duration is 50 time units which are not given as days so duration is not directly available.

Framework: ecological adaptability cycle = potential + resilience + connectivity
Justification: The study uses a three-dimensional framework described as the ecological adaptability cycle with the components potential resilience connectivity. Resilience is not quantified as recovery after a defined disturbance but is treated as an indicator where continuous green trend of vegetation indicates stronger resilience. The methods state that pixels with significantly increased NDVI from 2010 to 2020 were used as resilient pixels and patches composed of these pixels were resilience patches. This uses NDVI change trend as a proxy for resilience rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a disturbance event. Connectivity is quantified using the Probability of Connectivity index dPC and potential is based on ecosystem services importance from soil conservation water yield habitat quality and carbon sequestration. The study area is the Yanhe River Basin with coordinates 36•27′–37•58′N and 108•41′–110•29′E and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation is Xi’an University of Science and Technology in China. The data are from the Yanhe River Basin in China. The study states grassland is the major land use type and primary ecological sources are predominantly located where grassland was the dominant land use type so the major habitat type is grassland. Because the level of analysis is landscape patches and networks and the task specifies taxon is NA when level is landscape the taxon is NA. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because measurement is inferred and resilience is derived from NDVI trend rather than response to a disturbance.

The paper states that it developed and used a model and that empirical data were not used. Fire is imposed in the model as an external process with fire size and fire free interval as control parameters and fires occur as a Poisson process and all trees within the fire perimeter are killed. The analysis quantifies outcomes using equilibrium adult tree density and extirpation thresholds across fire size and fire free interval and fire rotation. Resilience is evaluated by comparing where total density in the mixed forest exceeded that of monospecific forests and by persistence across disturbance regimes. The study defines resilience as the ability to avoid qualitative state changes and it operationalizes qualitative change using extirpation thresholds where a viable population becomes locally extinct and it assumes failure to regenerate if tree density was less than 50 per hectare. This corresponds to a threshold to an alternative state which matches latitude. The target variable used in the resilience comparison is total adult tree density at equilibrium. The focal organisms are tree species and regeneration strategies including lodgepole pine and quaking aspen which are plants. The model runs each simulation for 1500 years and discards the first 500 years then computes equilibrium density during the remaining 1000 years which is 1500 years total or 547500 days. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin Madison in the United States. The case study system is subalpine forests in Yellowstone National Park but the data are simulated and no coordinates are provided.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigour + organisation + resilience + services
Justification: The study used the Vigour –Organisation –Resilience-Services model to analyse ecosystem health and states that EH encompasses ecosystem vigour organisation resilience and service functions and that the ecosystem health index is derived from the normalised geometric mean of V O R and S. Ecosystem resilience is defined as the ability of an ecosystem to maintain its structural stability under human activities and the paper states that the vegetation recovery coefficient and resistance index can be determined based on relevant studies and values associated with the respective urban expansion areas. Resilience is operationalised as fixed values by land use because Table 2 reports resilience values for land use classes such as cropland forest shrubland grassland water bodies bare land and IPS. Because resilience is assigned from land use categories rather than measured as recovery dynamics in a time series the measurement type is inferred. The study area is Chengdu in Sichuan province China and the first author affiliation is Chengdu University of Technology China. The study area coordinates are given as 102 54 to 104 53 E and 30 05 to 31 26 N and the average coordinate is reported. The work uses land use simulation with the PLUS model and scenario simulation for 2035 and also uses field observation via remote sensing and spatial datasets including Landsat derived land use and NDVI and MODIS NPP.

The study imposed a summer drought using rainout shelters from 9 July to 6 September 2010 and quantified community stability using above-ground biomass harvested in early July and early September 2009 2010 and 2011. The Methods define resilience as the ratio of total biomass after the drought year 2011 to total biomass before the drought year 2009. The Introduction states resilience combines resistance and recovery to provide a measure of community stability. Resistance and recovery were also calculated as biomass ratios relative to pre-drought biomass July 2010. The target variable used to quantify resistance recovery and resilience is community above-ground biomass including regrowth biomass and total biomass. The work is a field experiment in a Swiss Jura grassland pasture at 46 50 50 N and 6 34 30 E. The first author affiliation is EPFL in Lausanne Switzerland. The disturbance includes manipulated climatic drought and manipulated biotic removal of subordinate species. The drought is treated as a discrete event with a defined start and end and recovery is assessed afterward. The observation window for resilience spans from the first biomass harvest in early July 2009 to the last harvest in early September 2011 which is about two years.

The study calculated drought resilience indices from individual tree ring width series using formulas including Resilience = RWpostDr/RWpreDr and Recovery = RWpostDr/RWDr with RWpreDr and RWpostDr defined as mean tree ring width of four years before and after the extreme drought year of 2001 and RWDr defined as tree ring width in 2001. The disturbance was identified as the most extreme drought year in 2001 characterized by high temperatures low precipitation and low relative humidity using climate data. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event year of 2001 with recovery assessed in the four years after 2001 which matches a pulse framing. Resilience quantification uses ratios of post drought to pre drought ring width which is a recovery degree measure rather than a rate or time. The target variable used in the resilience indices is tree ring width which is a growth variable and indices were calculated for each tree indicating an individual level. The approach is field observation because tree ring cores were collected in natural forests during summer of 2019 and analyzed with dendrochronology and wood anatomy. The first author affiliation is Northeast Forestry University Harbin China indicating institution country CN. The study area is in the central part of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region in northern China and sampling site coordinates are given in Table 1 as 109.38E 40.75N 111.14E 40.91N and 112.29E 40.64N with an average of N40.77 E110.94 and a location range computed from the farthest site. The habitat is secondary coniferous broad leaved mixed forests described as Chinese pine broadleaf mixed forests. The taxa are tree species Pinus tabuliformis Betula platyphylla and Quercus liaotungensis which are plants. The observation period for resilience analysis is defined as 1980 to 2018 corresponding to overlapping period of tree ring width chronologies and climate data which is 58 years or 21170 days.

The study directly measured the grass layer using repeated surveys that counted and identified all grass individuals within 120 permanent 1 m2 plots before the first fire in 2015 and again after seven annual mid dry season fires in 2022. The authors state that after seven annual burns community composition remained unchanged and grass density doubled evidencing the grass layer resilience to repeated fires. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework in the introduction methods or discussion even though the text mentions test the resilience sensu Dakos and Kéfi 2022. Resilience is quantified by comparing before and after states using a log response ratio effect size approach for density and by testing whether community composition remained similar after the fire regime. The target variables used in the core resilience claim are grass density per m2 and community composition based on species abundances. The biological level is community because the variables are community properties and community composition across multiple grass species. The approach is a field experiment because plots were burned every year from 2015 to 2021 as prescribed fires and vegetation was sampled in permanent plots. The first author affiliation is Universidade Estadual de Campinas in Brazil. The field site is Estação Ecológica de Santa Bárbara São Paulo Brazil with coordinates 22°46′33″ to 22°50′30″S and 49°10′27″ to 49°15′36″W. The location is reported as the midpoint of the coordinate ranges. The habitat is grassland because plots were grassland patches classified locally as campo sujo with canopy cover below 5 percent. The taxon is Plantae because the subjects are grasses. The disturbance is manipulated fire because plots were burned every year and fire was ensured to be the only factor affecting the vegetation. The disturbance pattern is press because annual burns were maintained across the study period with no defined recovery interval between burns. The observation duration is from March 2015 to March 2022 which is seven years or 2555 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: resilience = time to return to stable state
Justification: The abstract states that time series Landsat imagery during 1994–2015 were used to observe forest recovery of Wu-Ling fire scars in Taiwan. The introduction defines engineering resilience as the time to return disturbed ecosystems back to stable state and then states that in this study engineering resilience is defined as tc and stable state is assumed as the level of pre-disturbed or undisturbed reference area. The methods predict time of complete recovery tc using curve fitting of forest recovery trajectories to an exponential decay function using time series of NBR. The study quantifies resilience as tc which is explicitly described as time of complete recovery and indicator of post-fire resilience. NBR is described as sensitive to chlorophyll content of live vegetation and water content in vegetation and soils and the paper uses NBR trajectories and derived indices BRR and tc to represent recovery which is an ecosystem level spectral index proxy of vegetation condition. The disturbance is wildfire with historical records showing burn dates and the study analyzes post-fire recovery which treats fire as discrete events. The first author affiliation includes National Chung Hsing University in Taichung Taiwan. The study area is Wu-Ling farm in Taiwan and the fire scars are in central Taiwan. The paper provides no explicit coordinates so the location is approximated from Wu-Ling farm. The observation window spans from 06/11/1994 to 16/11/2015 as listed in the Landsat scenes table. The main vegetation is pine plantation forest with named tree and understory species indicating plants in a forest habitat.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity of an ecosystem to resist and recover from stress. The methods describe a 5-year experiment with rainout shelters set up for around 75 days from mid-June to the end of August that excluded 100% precipitation and created a warmer and drier microclimate. The study quantified a tipping point as an abrupt loss of resilience when the extreme water stress index exceeded 36.7 ± 1.5 mm 10 day−1 characterized by a reduction of spring plant cover below 50% and a decreased ANPP in rainy years. The tipping point is a threshold beyond which the original structure and function cannot be maintained which matches latitude. The resilience index RES was calculated as the ratio between plant response under drought versus ambient treatment and significant differences to 1 full recovery were tested by t-test. The key response used to identify the tipping point was spring plant cover assessed in each quadrat at the end of spring. The site is located at the INRAE La Fage station southern France with coordinates 43°55′N 3°05′E. The first author affiliation list includes CEFE Univ Montpellier Montpellier France. The disturbance is manipulated summer drought imposed by rainout shelters. The disturbance is treated as a discrete summer manipulation each year with shelters removed afterward which is a pulse. The experiment ran over five successive years which corresponds to about 1825 days between first and last measurements. The habitat is described as a native grass-shrub rangeland which is best matched to grassland. The study subjects are plant communities dominated by native perennial graminoids forbs and shrubs which are Plantae.

The study states that it quantified “the growth resilience capacity after drought events” using dendrochronology and “standardized ring width series” which are direct observations of growth over time. The Methods define “resilience Rs” as “the ability of trees to reach pre-disturbance growth levels” and also define resistance and recovery as separate indices. The drought events used for resilience calculations are explicitly identified as “2005 and 2012” based on SPEI and described as “dry years in both study areas”. Resilience indices were calculated “at the individual level” and the sampling measured “radial growth” via ring widths and basal area increment. The disturbance is climatic because the focal events are “drought events” and drought severity is assessed with SPEI. The disturbance is unmanipulated because drought years are identified from climate data rather than imposed experimentally. The disturbance is treated as pulse because indices are calculated “considering the 2005 and 2012 droughts” with defined “pre- and post-disturbance periods” and a “maximum recovery period”. The quantification is recovery degree because resilience Rs is defined as reaching “pre-disturbance growth levels” and recovery period is separately defined as time to recover. The target variable is growth because indices are calculated “from standardized ring width series” describing radial growth. The approach is field observation because samples were collected in the field and growth was reconstructed from tree rings. The first author affiliation is “Instituto Pirenaico de Ecología (IPE-CSIC), Zaragoza, Spain”. The field sites are in “north-eastern Spain” with coordinates given for Alcubierre and Valcuerna and the provided location is the average of those coordinates. The observation window for growth analyses is “1980–2020” which is 40 years corresponding to 14600 days using 365 days per year.

The study states it developed an assessment framework for tourism socio-ecological system resilience using an indicator system and applied an improved TOPSIS model to compute a TSESR value. The framework is explicitly described as encompassing four key domains resistance recovery adaptation and renewal. The indicators in Table 1 are proxy variables such as per capita water resources normalized vegetation index PM2.5 per capita GDP tourism revenue numbers of scenic spots and higher education indicators rather than direct measurements of recovery after a defined disturbance. The results report resilience levels and spatiotemporal variations of the computed index from 2010 to 2020 rather than observed system recovery dynamics after a disturbance. The study area is Xinjiang China and the case study covers 14 prefecture-level units. The first author affiliation is Xinjiang Institute of Ecology and Geography Chinese Academy of Sciences in Urumqi China. The data sources are statistical yearbooks and public datasets indicating an observational non-experimental approach. The paper provides no explicit coordinates so the location is approximated to the center of Xinjiang. The subject is a tourism socio-ecological system so no biological taxon is directly quantified for resilience. Disturbances are discussed generally in the introduction and study area description but are not used as imposed or analyzed disturbance events for resilience quantification so disturbance fields are NA.

The study uses a virtual lake created with the ecosystem model PCLake and states that it capitalizes on data simulated using the aquatic ecosystem model PCLake. The methods state that the authors ran PCLake for 50 year long eutrophication and oligotrophication scenarios and extracted the time series of all model state variables. Resilience is operationalized through early warning indicators that are described as assessing loss of temporal resilience and linked to critical slowing down characterized by an increased recovery time after a minor perturbation and increasing autocorrelation at lag 1. The early warning indicators quantified are AR1 SD and DR calculated as trends using Kendall tau on residual time series. Because AR1 is explicitly tied to recovery time and critical slowing down and is used as an early warning indicator the quantification corresponds to recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience quantification are ecosystem state variables such as chlorophyll a transparency Secchi depth and oxygen and the paper treats these as water quality and productivity related indicators so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The virtual lake is described as a shallow lake and the habitat is a freshwater lake which fits Wetland in the provided habitat options. The study subjects are model compartments and the paper describes a virtual lake and virtual data so the taxon is Virtual. The disturbance driver is imposed as linearly increasing or decreasing phosphorus load over 50 years and stochastic temperature fluctuations are added as perturbations so the disturbance types are model resource and model climatic. The phosphorus load change is applied gradually over the full 50 years so the disturbance pattern is press. The observation duration is 50 years which equals 18250 days.

The introduction states that whole stands were perturbed by harvesting in a pulse experiment and recovery of carabid assemblages was monitored one two and five years postharvest. The methods describe an operational scale experiment with harvesting treatments of dispersed green tree retention including clearcut and multiple retention levels plus uncut controls applied in winter 1998 to 1999. Ground beetles were sampled with pitfall traps during May through August of 1999 2000 and 2004 and identified to species and plot level catch rate of 59 species was analyzed as Bray Curtis dissimilarities. The methods state that significant differences in pairwise comparisons between harvesting treatments and control stands over time were interpreted as a measure of resistance and potential resilience of ground beetle composition to the effects of harvesting. The discussion states that recovery was judged relative to beetle compositions found within uncut stands rather than paired pretreatment stands. The study site is near Peace River Alberta Canada with coordinates 5684601300N 118 82202800W. The first author affiliation is Universite du Quebec a Montreal in Canada. The disturbance is harvesting which is a human use of biological resources. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because harvesting occurred in winter 1998 to 1999 and responses were measured after. The observation window spans from 1999 to 2004 which is five years.

The study states it analyses an exceptionally long time series and reports a 35 years survey of annually sampled permanent quadrats treated as individual plot time series. It explicitly analyses pulse disturbances of climatic origin described as extremely dry or wet years and a press disturbance of habitat degradation at the landscape scale. Resilience is defined as the ability to preserve community composition and structure in time and the methods quantify temporal turnover and beta diversity components over time using Sörensen Simpson nestedness Raup Crick and Bray Curtis. The paper also states that local community resilience is measured as the stability of communities over short time steps of annual measures and that annual versus decadal turnover gives a measure for community stabilising mechanisms. In the results it reports that functional group composition returned to the same as at the beginning and states in terms of recovery to initial conditions there are definitively resilience mechanisms at play which supports recovery degree quantification. The target variables used for these resilience measures are community composition and its turnover and nestedness components derived from presence absence and abundance data of higher plants angiosperms. The field site is the Thier de Lanaye calcareous grassland complex near Maastricht at the Belgian Dutch border and the plots are within the same grassland complex. The first author affiliation is Irstea in Lyon Villeurbanne France. The disturbance period is identified from meteorological precipitation variability and habitat degradation is treated as a press disturbance. The observation duration is 35 years converted to 12775 days.

The study quantified resilience using Markov models derived from repeated censuses of fixed points in the middle intertidal community of Tatoosh Island Washington USA. It states that manipulated models predicted that extinction of several species particularly predatory birds affects the resilience of the system as assessed by return time. Return time was calculated from the eigenvalues as 1 divided by the subdominant eigenvalue and was compared as an index of the effects of different ecological states on community resilience. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is community composition because the model projects the proportion of points characterized by each ecological state and return time is derived from the community transition matrix. The approach includes field observation because transition probabilities were derived from repeated censuses on transects and quadrats and includes modeling and simulation because resilience was assessed from eigenanalysis and simulations of time varying matrices. The first author affiliation is University of Chicago in the United States. The field site is Tatoosh Island Washington USA which is a rocky intertidal marine habitat. The disturbance is species extinction implemented by altering the model to reflect species extinction including gull extinction which is a biotic disturbance imposed in the model. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because the model evaluates return time from a perturbation and species removal is implemented as a discrete change in the transition matrix. Data collection ran through May 1993 to May 1998 which is five years or 1825 days.

The study used two decades 2000–2020 of annual net primary productivity data from early successional grassland communities. It quantified resistance as the distance from mean during an event year using perturbation year ANPP and long term average ANPP. It quantified recovery as the return rate to mean following an event using post perturbation year ANPP. It quantified long term invariability as the long term mean subplot ANPP divided by the long term standard deviation of subplot ANPP. The introduction states that resilience is multi dimensional and that they quantify aspects of resilience resistance recovery and invariability. Precipitation events were identified as drought and wet events based on SPEI and were naturally occurring. The methods describe annual peak biomass harvest used to calculate ANPP and species relative abundances at the Kellogg Biological Station LTER site in Hickory Corners Michigan. The site coordinate is given as 42°24′ N and −85°22′ W. The first author affiliation is Indiana University Bloomington Indiana USA. The disturbance is precipitation anomalies droughts and wet events which are climatic and treated as discrete event years with recovery assessed in the year after a perturbation.

Framework: post-fire resilience = capacity to tolerate fire without shifting to an alternative ecosystem state
Justification: The introduction defines post-fire resilience as the capacity to tolerate fire without shifting to an alternative ecosystem state. The methods quantify post-fire tree seedling establishment using field plots in stand-replacing fire patches from 11 wildfires that occurred between 1994 and 2003 and field sampling was conducted from June to August 2013. The response variable in the models was the total number of seedlings present at the time of sampling divided by the number years since the fire and standardized to seedlings ha-1 year-1 which is a density based measure. The study tests how establishment rates varied with post-fire drought severity and distance to seed source after stand-replacing fires which evaluates how much the system deviates in regeneration under disturbance rather than measuring time to return to a prior state. The study area is in the US Northern Rockies and provides coordinates for Glacier National Park and the Greater Yellowstone ecosystem. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin in the USA. The disturbance is unmanipulated wildfire and post-fire drought severity during the first four post-fire years. The fires are discrete events with years of occurrence and the study examines post-fire response which matches a pulse framing. Observation duration spans from the earliest fire year 1994 to sampling in 2013 which is 19 years or 6935 days.

The methods state that they used standardized impact and absolute recovery rates as proxies for ecological resilience to drought. The recovery rates were quantified as the absolute change in grass greenness per day after each dry season. Grass greenness was measured monthly during the dry and wet seasons of 2018 and 2019 in permanent plots under canopies and paired open plots. The disturbance is seasonal drought described as the dry season and a severe dry season of 2019 with reduced rainfall. The study region is within the municipality of Tizimín Yucatán in the southeast of Mexico. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University and Research Wageningen The Netherlands. The approach is a correlational study under natural conditions in cattle farms. The target variable used for resilience quantification is grass greenness which the methods describe as a response variable and a measure of grass physiological condition.
